cards.text: The {{c1::char}} data type is an integral type designed to hold a {{c2::character}}.
Character literals are written using {{c1::single quotes}}: char c{{c2::{'a'}}}

cards.question: What's the correct way to initialize a char?
cards.explanation: Characters use single quotes ('a'). Double quotes ("a") are for strings.

cards.front: What is the size of a char in C++?

cards.question: What does this print?
cards.explanation: std::cout prints char variables as characters.

cards.text: Characters are actually stored as {{c1::integers}} using the {{c2::ASCII}} encoding (or Unicode).

cards.question: What does this print?
cards.explanation: The ASCII value of 'a' is 97. Casting to int prints the numeric value instead of the character.

cards.text: Special characters are written using {{c1::escape sequences}} that start with a {{c2::backslash}} (\).

cards.front: What are the most common escape sequences in C++?
cards.back:
• \n - newline
  • \t - tab
  • \' - single quote
  • \" - double quote
  • \\ - backslash

cards.question: What does this print?
cards.explanation: \n is the newline escape sequence, which moves to the next line.

cards.text: Whether char is signed or unsigned is {{c1::implementation-defined}}. Use {{c2::signed char}} or {{c3::unsigned char}} to be explicit.

cards.explanation:
When using char:
  • Use char for single characters only
  • Use std::string for text
  • Don't use char for small integers (use int)
  • Be aware of signedness issues with non-ASCII
examples: int count{ 5 };          // good: not char for number
